title: Zonotopal Algebras, Configuration Spaces, and More
speaker: Colin William Crowley
abstract:
We consider the space of configurations of n points in the three-sphere S3, some of which may coincide and some of which may not, up to the free and transitive action of SU(2) on S3. We prove that the cohomology ring with rational coefficients is isomorphic to an internal zonotopal algebra, which is a combinatorially defined ring appearing independently in the work of Holtz and Ron and of Ardila and Postnikov. We use zonotopal algebras to prove a conjecture of Moseley, Proudfoot, and Young in 2016 about the cohomology of these configuration spaces. Along the way, we also give a formula for the equivariant K-polynomial of a matroid Schubert variety with respect to a finite symmetry group.
Based on joint work with Galen Dorpalen-Barry, André Henriques, and Nicholas Proudfoot.

proof_stages.label: Graph configuration quotient
proof_stages.summary: The space X_gamma is defined as SU(2)-valued graph configurations with adjacent vertices distinct, modulo simultaneous left translation, and the main goal is announced as cohomology-ring presentations over Q and Z.

proof_stages.label: Motivating representation and zonotopal targets
proof_stages.summary: The study is motivated by a conjectural identification with hypertoric intersection cohomology, by the previously defined internal zonotopal algebra, and by equivariant K-polynomials of arrangement Schubert varieties.

proof_stages.label: Elementary cases and deletion-contraction Betti recursion
proof_stages.summary: Loop and bridge cases provide base behavior, and for an edge that is neither a loop nor a bridge a deletion-contraction decomposition and long exact sequence give a recursive computation of Betti numbers as the broken-circuit/Tutte polynomial specialization.

proof_stages.label: Co-graphic zonotope and internal zonotopal algebra
proof_stages.summary: After orienting the graph, the co-graphic vector configuration and zonotope are built from edge cohomology classes, and the Ardila-Postnikov/Holtz-Ron internal zonotopal algebra is presented with its Hilbert series matching the Betti-number formula.

proof_stages.label: Rational cohomology presentation
proof_stages.summary: The first main theorem identifies H^*(X_gamma;Q), with degrees halved, with the internal zonotopal algebra, implying vanishing of odd cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Integral filtered-ring refinement
proof_stages.summary: Filtered rings of polynomial functions on internal lattice points are introduced, their associated graded defines the integral internal zonotopal algebra, and the second main theorem identifies this algebra with H^*(X_gamma;Z).

proof_stages.label: Equivariant localization proof mechanism
proof_stages.summary: A coordinatewise circle action on X_gamma is introduced, the equivariant cohomology is identified with the Rees algebra of the lattice-point filtration, and restriction to fixed points explains why internal lattice points control the answer.

proof_stages.label: Inverse systems and hypertoric intersection cohomology
proof_stages.summary: For a general rational coordinate subspace, the Macaulay inverse system of the zonotopal quotient is related by a natural isomorphism to hypertoric intersection cohomology/trial algebras, using rigidity of sheaves on lattices of flats and proving the earlier representation conjecture.

examples.name: Complete graph and hidden symmetric-group action
examples.note: For the complete graph, fixing one SU(2) coordinate identifies X_gamma with configurations of n-1 labeled points in R^3, revealing the hidden S_n action that motivated the SU(2) construction and the conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker fixes one vertex of the complete graph to the identity, identifies the remaining choices with distinct points in R^3, and explains how this yields the hidden S_n action.

examples.name: Cone-point graphs as R^3 graph configuration spaces
examples.note: When a graph has a cone vertex, fixing that vertex turns X_gamma into a graph configuration space in R^3, connecting the definition to the already known Cordovil/Varchenko-Gelfand presentation.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explains the cone-point mechanism by using the quotient to set the cone vertex to 1 and identifying the other vertex labels in SU(2) minus that point with R^3 configurations.

examples.name: Integer-valued binomial polynomial basis
examples.note: On a one-dimensional finite lattice set, the functions 1, binomial(x-1,1), and binomial(x-1,2) demonstrate how integer-valued polynomials with rational coefficients produce divided-power leading terms in the associated graded.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker computes the filtered ring in a concrete lattice-point example using integer-valued binomial polynomials and identifies the associated graded leading terms.
